title: Costa Rica - National Report (Cycle 4, Session 47)
text:
United Nations A
/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
General Assembly
Distr.: General
29 August 2024
English
Original: Spanish
Human Rights Council
Working Group on the Universal Periodic Review
Forty-seventh session
Geneva, 4–15 November 2024
National report submitted pursuant to Human Rights
Council resolutions 5/1 and 16/21
*
Costa Rica
* The present document is being issued without formal editing.
GE.24-15566 (E) 120924 120924

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
I. Introduction
A. Overview
1. Costa Rica hereby submits its fourth national report for the universal periodic review,
thereby reaffirming its commitment to respect, protect and promote human rights.1
2. The country’s legal framework is constantly being reviewed and updated in order to
protect human rights more effectively, including by creating protection and enforcement
mechanisms, and this ongoing improvement is reflected in its external activities in support
of the promotion of standards that enhance the dignity and well-being of all persons, without
discrimination of any form.
3. Like all States, Costa Rica faces continuing challenges, including those typical of an
upper-middle-income developing country in terms of international cooperation, as well as
problems linked to persistent poverty and inequalities, violence of all forms, including violent
crime, and other social, economic and institutional challenges.
4. At the different levels of government, Costa Rica therefore prioritizes strategies
designed to optimize existing resources, coordinate general and specialized care services, and
channel the latter towards vulnerable groups and others requiring special protection. It is
committed to developing and implementing innovative solutions with a focus on human
security.
5. The fourth cycle of the universal periodic review, which makes it possible to define
shared goals for improving the State’s practices, is seen both as a State responsibility and as
an opportunity to reflect as a country on the human rights situation.
B. Report coordination and methodology
6. Since 2011, the Inter-Institutional Commission for the Monitoring and
Implementation of International Human Rights Obligations2 has had a strategic role in the
coordination of the universal periodic review and other processes related to the universal and
inter-American human rights system.
7. This report is the fruit of the most inclusive consultation process related to the
universal periodic review undertaken to date; under the leadership of the Inter-Institutional
Commission, 38 institutions 3 of the executive, judicial and legislative branches of
government reported on the State’s activities in the field of human rights.
8. Civil society consultations – a State responsibility recognized as helping to ensure
transparency and accountability in addition to constituting a valuable opportunity to build
trust and revitalize coordinated efforts in the area of human rights – were part of the process.
9. The point of departure for these consultations was an update to the register of the
Permanent Consultation Entity – an entity attached to the Inter-Institutional Commission that
is responsible for ensuring civil society participation – in April and May 2024.4 The
Permanent Consultation Entity constituted the main base of organizations involved in the
consultations, which had two components: the submission of the preliminary report for public
consultation and a call for written submissions.
10. The methodology was enhanced on the basis of input from members of the
Inter-Institutional Commission, representatives of civil society, the Office of the
Ombudsperson, the Office of the United Nations Resident Coordinator in Costa Rica and the
United Nations Special Rapporteur on the right to freedom of expression and peaceful
association.5 Consultation with civil society needs to be broadened still further in the future.
11. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs, as coordinator of the Inter-Institutional Commission,
is grateful for the input of various civil society organizations. Their contributions were
considered while the report was being prepared, lending depth and transparency to the
activities reported.
2 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
II. Normative and institutional framework
A. Main advances
12. Costa Rica is a democratic, free, independent, multiethnic and multicultural republic6
with a system of government based on the separation of powers; it is a social welfare State
governed by the rule of law.
13. The Constitution, as the supreme body of law in the Costa Rican legal system,
establishes that international human rights provisions take precedence over national
legislation. Constitutional jurisprudence has even established that, whenever they grant
greater rights or guarantees, they may be above the Constitution.7
14. The public institutions and bodies established under the three branches of government
(the legislature, the executive branch and the judiciary) and the Supreme Electoral Tribunal,
the efficacy of which is well recognized, have specific constitutional and legal functions
related to the promotion and protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms.
15. Costa Rica has ratified nine international human rights treaties and nine optional
protocols and has agreed to be bound by seven of the individual communications procedures.8
In addition, the country has given all international human rights mechanisms an open,
standing invitation to visit the country.
16. In compliance with its international human rights obligations, the Government
submitted the following reports during the most recent reporting period: combined fifth and
sixth periodic reports to the Committee on the Rights of the Child; combined third and fourth
periodic reports to the Committee against Torture; initial report to the Committee on
Enforced Disappearances; eighth periodic report to the Committee on the Elimination of
Discrimination against Women; combined second and third periodic reports to the
Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities; and the first visit of the Subcommittee
on Prevention of Torture.
17. In the last four years, the country underwent constitutional and legal reforms of great
importance for human rights, which, organized by subject matter, are described in the
following sections.
18. Since 2020, Costa Rica has been undergoing a historical change in terms of the
protection of human rights, including those of the sexually diverse population, following the
issue of Advisory Opinion OC-24/17 of the Inter-American Court of Human Rights, which
the Government requested on 18 May 2016.9
19. The Constitutional Chamber of the Supreme Court, invoking Advisory Opinion
OC-24/17, ruled that the prohibition of same-sex marriage enshrined in the Family Code was
unconstitutional. Marriage between persons of the same sex became lawful in Costa Rica on
26 May 2020.10
20. The ruling of the Constitutional Chamber marked the start of a new stage in the
development of the protection framework that continues today through domestic efforts to
protect the rights of individuals irrespective of their sexual orientation or gender identity.
B. Institutional and public policy development
21. As a member of the Human Rights Council for the period 2023–2025, the country
promotes a cross-cutting vision of human rights using political dialogue and coordination
with all stakeholders as a tool.
22. Costa Rica has spearheaded the recognition of new human rights standards, including
resolutions adopted by the Human Rights Council and the General Assembly recognizing the
human right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment;11 the declaration of the
International Day for People of African Descent; and the establishment of the Permanent
Forum on People of African Descent.12
GE.24-15566 3

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
23. Costa Rica has a robust institutional architecture dedicated to safeguarding the rights
of population groups in need of special protection as well as numerous inter-agency
coordination bodies. They are discussed in more detail in the following sections. The
principles of mainstreaming, intersectionality and territoriality are reflected in the design of
public policies related to human rights, the aim being to guarantee their application by all
institutions and thus reinforce the focus on ensuring that their approaches are complementary.
24. Costa Rica became a full member of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and
Development (OECD)13 in 2021, thereby opening up new opportunities for improvement in
multiple areas of sustainable development, public sector innovation, the design of
forward-thinking public policies, the compilation of statistical information for
decision-making, and more extensive participation by non-governmental actors.
25. The National Development and Public Investment Plan 2023–2026,14 which is the
country’s principal road map towards sustainable development, has for the first time been
linked to the Sustainable Development Goals and the Montevideo Consensus on Population
and Development in Latin America and the Caribbean; it applies OECD standards and
recommendations. Gender, human rights, territorial development and risk management
perspectives are also mainstreamed in the Plan.
26. Work towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals is also under way at the
cantonal level, through the Network of Cantons Promoting the Goals, which currently
includes more than half of the local governments. As a result of this work, Costa Rica has
submitted six voluntary local reports, and in July 2024 it submitted its third voluntary national
report to the high-level political forum on sustainable development.
27. In 2021, Costa Rica adopted the National Strategic Plan,15 which sets out a long-term
vision that incorporates the country’s long-standing commitment to human rights.16
28. In 2024, the Office of the Ombudsperson was reaccredited as the country’s national
human rights institution in the top category (category A status), having been deemed
compliant with the principles relating to the status of national institutions for the promotion
and protection of human rights (the Paris Principles). The Office of the Ombudsperson, which
has direct and independent access to the United Nations bodies that monitor fulfilment of the
human rights obligations assumed by the country, has the authority to provide any
information it deems appropriate in the independent exercise of its powers.
29. The Costa Rican Institute of Electricity, a conglomerate of State-owned companies
dedicated to electrification and telecommunications with a social and environmental focus,
was the first State-owned company to assume, in 2023, a commitment to human rights due
diligence,17 which entails implementing ongoing due diligence procedures in all its
operations, supply chains and business relationships. This commitment, made in accordance
with the Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights and OECD standards, was a
milestone for the country.18
30. The contribution of civil society to the development of public policies and the
implementation of actions, from the provision of services to the training of public officials,
was key in these and the other developments described below. Costa Rica reaffirms the
importance of their participation, as well as the State’s obligation to guarantee their autonomy
and security.
C. National statistics system
31. The 2019 Act on the National Statistics System19 was a step forward in the
harmonization and expansion of statistical production, giving the National Statistics and
Census Institute a clearer mandate as the lead agency and the resources it needed to fulfil this
mandate.
32. The Institute reviews and updates the Sustainable Development Goals Indicators
Monitoring System on an ongoing basis,20 and, as a complement, it has developed a system
of cantonal indicators. The latter system was essential to the development of the Costa Rican
National Goals Strategy 2023–2030, the main planning instrument of the 2030 Agenda for
4 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
Sustainable Development, which has made it possible to define 55 national goals in line with
the Sustainable Development Goals and the associated annual monitoring system.
33. Significant efforts have been made to improve data disaggregation with a view to
achieving a better analysis of the situation of specific population groups. In 2022, for
example, technical guidelines for incorporating an ethnic and racial perspective in the
production of official statistics was published;21 a framework of reference for taking account
of the LGTBI+ population in the production and dissemination of official statistics was also
developed.22
D. Judiciary
34. Since the adoption of the Brasilia Regulations Regarding Access to Justice for
Vulnerable People, updated in 2019, the judiciary has had institutional policies and guidelines
specific to different population groups (for example, LGTBIQ+ persons, persons of African
descent, Indigenous Peoples, children and adolescents, women, older persons, persons with
disabilities, minors in conflict with the law and persons deprived of liberty) in order to
safeguard their human rights and access to justice.
35. The Commission on Access to Justice and its subcommissions work in coordination
with the Judicial Training College and other training institutions to ensure that judicial
personnel receive human rights training and sensitization on an ongoing basis.23
36. Following the adoption of the Act on Access to Justice for the Indigenous Peoples of
Costa Rica in 2018, a plan to develop an institutional policy for access to justice for
Indigenous Peoples was initiated that is currently at the final diagnostic stage. The plan was
designed in line with International Labour Organization (ILO) Indigenous and Tribal Peoples
Convention, 1989 (No. 169), with an emphasis on guaranteeing the right to prior
consultation.24
37. In circular No. 188-2019, the Supreme Court set out the 19 lines of action to be
considered by judicial units in order to guarantee the rights and particular needs of Indigenous
Peoples and ensure that their world view and culture are respected and that their needs are
reflected in institutional processes and actions.25
38. Within the Public Prosecution Service, the Office of the Special Prosecutor for
Indigenous Affairs is the body responsible for taking action to address crimes involving
Indigenous Peoples that goes beyond simply combating crime. The Public Defence Service,
for its part, is responsible for monitoring matters involving Indigenous communities in order
to ensure compliance with the procedural safeguards to which they are entitled.26
39. The institutional policy for access to justice for people of African descent and the
related action plan address the particular needs of this population group with the support of
other judicial bodies.27
40. Since 2021, the National Commission to Improve the Administration of Justice, a
commission convened by the judicial authorities, has been leading efforts to define sexual
orientation and gender identity variables, include them in institutional management systems
and develop a plan for the collection of data on LGBTIQ persons in consultation with
members of this segment of the population.28
41. The judiciary has a road map, the product of more than eight years of adaptation,
alignment and harmonization of its work with the 2030 Agenda, for the achievement of the
Sustainable Development Goals. The road map constitutes an institutional commitment that
transcends the mere administration of justice to embrace a vision of sustainable development
and social well-being.
E. Legislative Assembly
42. The Legislative Assembly made a declaration to the effect that fulfilling the
Sustainable Development Goals was in the interests of the country. Accordingly, it works to
ensure that the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development is continually referenced,
GE.24-15566 5

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
monitored and evaluated, including by promoting the categorization of existing legislation
and new bills in accordance with the 17 Sustainable Development Goals.
43. Pursuant to the Constitution and national laws, the Office of the Comptroller General
of the Republic is the institution responsible for the oversight and effective use of State
resources. As part of its checks and controls, the Office conducts audits focused on the
efficiency of public services and associated issues that include monitoring levels of
compliance with the Sustainable Development Goals.29
44. Since 2022, the legislature has worked to ensure that all reports on technical services
include a gender impact assessment with recommendations to be addressed in future bills. In
2024, a technical information sheet was adopted to provide guidance for assessing projects
from a gender perspective.30
45. The mandate of the Specialist Commission on Employment and Disability, created in
2021, is to ensure that an appropriate number of vacancies to be filled in the public sector are
reserved for persons with disabilities, as required by law, which stipulates that the three
branches of government should reserve at least 5 per cent of vacancies for such persons.31
III. Action taken in follow-up to recommendations since 2019
A. Cross-cutting issues
1. Combating poverty
46. The fight against poverty and inequality is an ongoing challenge and a priority of
Costa Rican public policy. Accordingly, benefits of various types are being offered in order
to meet the needs of socially and financially vulnerable segments of the population; and
specifically to achieve qualitative improvements in their skills and to make alternative care
available for dependent persons and their caregivers.
47. The National Strategy for Employability and Human Talent32 sets out the framework
for State action, providing indicators for assessing the effective execution of the range of
State programmes, as well as for cooperation with the private sector and for conducting
reviews of local needs that make it possible to target training efforts and subsidies in a manner
more consistent with local market needs and with a human rights-based approach.33
48. The Inter-Agency Institute for Social Assistance, the expert body responsible for
social policy and combating poverty and extreme poverty, engages in various forms of action,
including action to promote the social mobility of households; the implementation of the
intercultural model of assistance for Indigenous territories,34 under which its institutional
vision is tailored to the cultural specificities of Indigenous Peoples; the allocation of
conditional cash transfers and other training allowances as well as the award of loans backed
by guarantees with financial intermediaries to fund productive activities.35
49. The development banking system has a fund called Inclusion Seed Capital that is
intended for businesses owned by persons from priority sectors, as defined in Act No. 8634,
under which the development banking system was established.36
50. As a result of these efforts, and even though the pandemic, in addition to affecting
public finances, led to an increase in poverty, a significant 4.4 per cent reduction in poverty
was achieved between 2020 and 2023.37 In 2023, the incidence of multidimensional poverty
was 11.9 per cent, lower than at any time since 2010.38
2. Equality and non-discrimination
51. Achieving equality and combating all forms of discrimination are a cross-cutting
objective for the entire State. Accordingly, State policies are designed to address the multiple
and interrelated forms of discrimination to which women, older persons, children,
adolescents, young persons and other population groups are subjected.
52. A significant step forward was taken with the adoption of Act No. 10175 on 25 April
2022, which establishes prison sentences of between 20 and 35 years for hate crimes,
6 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
including homicide, bodily harm and assault, motivated by the victim’s ethnicity, age,
religion, nationality, political opinion, disability or genetic characteristics, migration status,
sexual orientation or gender identity or expression.39
53. In 2021, the Act against Violence and Racism in Sport entered into force. The aim of
the Act is to raise awareness of, prevent and punish acts of violence, racism and
discrimination that are contrary to human dignity during official sporting events.40
54. The Government is in the process of updating the National Cultural Rights Policy to
cover the period 2025–2034. The Policy is designed to foster the creation, study,
management, production, promotion and distribution of forms of cultural expressions and to
protect the diversity of the country’s cultural heritage.
55. In 2024, Costa Rica became the first country in Latin America and the Caribbean to
launch a national strategy against hate speech and discrimination;41 the aim of the strategy is
to address the exponential growth of hate speech in the country, particularly hate speech
disseminated on social networks.42
56. Work has also begun on the development of a national strategy to ensure that the
inter-agency coordination of existing policies accords particular attention to the needs of
persons of African descent, Indigenous Peoples and migrants, in line with the National Policy
for a Society Free from Racism, Racial Discrimination, Xenophobia and Other Related Forms
of Intolerance 2014–2025.43
3. Human rights education
57. The country recognizes 10 December each year as Human Rights Day44 and has added
a clause to the Fundamental Act on Education to provide for the ongoing study of the
Universal Declaration of Human Rights.
58. State institutions are working to maximize their resources and build the capacities of
their personnel on an ongoing basis, fostering skills and knowledge in the area of human
rights among public servants and making a direct impact on institutional culture and the
provision of services.45
59. The National Police Academy offers a range of training courses including, as a
graduation requirement for all prospective police officers, a course on human rights and
gender equality. Between 2020 and 2024, more than 4,000 police officers took this course.46
4. Environment
60. Costa Rica has been a pioneer in conserving and protecting natural resources,
incorporating social considerations into environmental matters and mainstreaming
environmental and human rights, notably in article 50 of the Constitution, which establishes
the right of every person to a healthy and ecologically balanced environment. Nearly
30 per cent of Costa Rican territory has been officially protected, and these protected areas
account for 6 per cent of international biodiversity.
61. The country’s innovative National Action Plan on Gender Equality in Climate Action
2023–2025, which is without precedent, promotes action to enhance employability and
economic autonomy, capacity-building, risk management, the incorporation of an
intersectional gender perspective in climate action and the production of disaggregated
information.47
62. The National Risk Management Plan 2021–202548 provides a framework for the
creation of mechanisms to ensure adequate cross-programme coordination and thus
institutional intervention that is more focused, equitable and efficient, particularly in disaster
response and recovery. The main results can be viewed in the Monitoring and Follow-up
System associated with the Plan.49
63. Costa Rica has led the global campaign to establish a precautionary pause on seabed
mining, in recognition of the science, the precautionary principle and the need to safeguard
the seabed as the common heritage of humanity and the right to a clean, healthy and
sustainable environment.50
GE.24-15566 7

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
64. As one of the co-hosts of the upcoming United Nations Ocean Conference, Costa Rica
organized a high-level event on ocean action in June 2024, which concluded with the launch
of a declaration of peace for the ocean and a call to action entitled “Women Guardians of the
Ocean”.
65. The Central Bank of Costa Rica prepares environmental accounts of the physical and
economic value of natural resources and their importance for the nation’s wealth. The Central
Bank also produces indicators that make it possible to monitor the supply and use of natural
resources and their contribution to the country’s economic activity.51
5. Digital rights
66. Costa Rica has taken important steps towards recognizing and safeguarding digital
rights. However, in order to close the digital divide, one of the greatest challenges facing the
country in this area, much remains to be done. In 2023, 96.8 per cent of the population had
access to a mobile phone, and 81.7 per cent had an Internet connection at home. According
to data published by the International Telecommunication Union, Costa Rica is among the
countries with the largest number of cellular phone lines per inhabitant in the world.
67. These advances were reflected in the addition, in 2023, of a second paragraph to
article 24 of the Constitution,52 which recognizes the fundamental right of access to
telecommunications and information and communications technologies.
68. The National Telecommunications Development Plan 2022–2027 provides the
framework for various policies, including open government initiatives, the Digital
Transformation Strategy 2022–2027, the National Cybersecurity Strategy 2023–2027 and the
Policy for the Use of Digital Technologies in Education,53 making Costa Rica a leader in the
provision of digital public services in addition to serving to promote digital citizenship.
69. The audits for which the Office of the Comptroller General is responsible make
increasingly extensive use of technology to improve timeliness, flexibility and
decision-making processes.54
70. Under a project to facilitate preventive auditing in the public sector based on the use
of artificial intelligence, an automated monitoring system is being implemented to track the
execution of public infrastructure work using digital technologies and artificial intelligence
that will enable citizens to observe in real time how public funds are being used. In addition,
remote cameras will be used to monitor ongoing work and will generate alerts regarding
possible risks and weather conditions that might affect progress.
71. The National Online Safety Commission is responsible for the implementation of the
National Strategy for Preventing and Responding to Online Sexual Abuse and Exploitation
2021–2027, which serves to promote safe digital spaces, prevent risks derived from
indiscriminate access to inappropriate content and ensure that situations of abuse or
aggression online are addressed.
72. In 2021, 18.7 per cent of Indigenous households did not have Internet access. The
National Telecommunications Plan provides for coverage to be extended to the country’s
24 Indigenous territories by 2026; by 31 December 2023, coverage had reached 14 of these
territories.55 Community training needs in Indigenous territories are addressed through the
Intelligent Community Centres Programme, ensuring respect for Indigenous world views,
culture and local characteristics.
B. Civil and political rights
1. Human security and fundamental freedoms
73. As a neutral country and unarmed democracy, Costa Rica reaffirms its commitment
to peace, disarmament and controlling the trafficking and proliferation of weapons. At the
domestic level, Act No. 9682 and Act No. 9692 of 2019 constituted important advances in
terms of strengthening the regulation of weapons and providing greater protection for
women, given that firearms and knives are the weapons most commonly used in violence
against women, particularly femicides.56
8 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
74. The Weapons and Explosives Act, as amended,57 increased the penalties for unlawful
possession of weapons and prohibits the acquisition of ammunition other than for legally
registered weapons, in addition to providing for other measures to prevent the proliferation
of weapons. The Domestic Violence Act, as amended, and the Weapons and Explosives
Act,58 as amended, expanded the scope of measures to restrict the possession and ensure the
confiscation of firearms in situations of domestic violence, and authorize the police to
intervene to protect personal safety.59
75. For Costa Rica, the ultimate goal of arms control instruments will not be achieved
without the full and equal contribution of women and men. As a sign of this commitment,
the country’s Directorate General for Armaments and its departments are headed by women.
76. Costa Rica is at the forefront of advocacy for a total ban on autonomous weapons
systems. It hosted the Latin American and Caribbean Conference on the Social and
Humanitarian Impact of Autonomous Weapons Systems in 2023, at which the Belén
Communiqué60 was adopted, and actively contributed, as one of the sponsors, to the adoption
of a resolution on lethal autonomous weapons systems by the United Nations General
Assembly.61 The country advocates the adoption of a legally binding instrument that
regulates autonomous weapons systems, provides for a multidisciplinary approach and
ensures meaningful human control at all times.
77. The Government has amended article 181 of the Code of Criminal Procedure to
recognize, in line with the recommendations of the Committee against Torture, the
inadmissibility of any evidence obtained through torture, ill-treatment, coercion, threat,
deception or undue interference.62
2. Participation in public and political life
78. With regard to the exercise of the right to peaceful protest in general, all
demonstrations or strikes are policed by means of a police alert order or, failing that, an
execution order, in which the need to respect all demonstrators is emphasized; and the police
forces are instructed to maintain dialogue in order to safeguard the bodily integrity of
civilians and police officers and respect the constitutional right to demonstrate.63
79. For several decades, Costa Rica has been leading efforts to ensure the equal
participation of women in all spheres of life, particularly in politics, and, as of May 2024, it
had achieved equal representation in the Legislative Assembly, with women holding
49 per cent of seats (27 congresswomen) and men 51 per cent (28).64
80. The country continues to work to guarantee parity at all levels, including at the
municipal level, where this work has been facilitated by amendments made to the Municipal
Code in 2023.65 In addition, the Act to Prevent, Address, Punish and Eradicate Violence
against Women in Politics, which reflects the constitutional principle of equality before the
law, was adopted in 2022.66
81. As a result of these and other affirmative actions, the country has achieved parity in
the composition of municipal councils; in addition, after the most recent municipal elections,
held in 2024, the number of female mayors rose from 7 to 22, a number equivalent to
27 per cent of all the country’s mayors.67
82. The National Institute for Women engages in a wide range of activities that include
advocacy for the political participation of women, free from all forms of violence, with
institutions and associations, political parties and social organizations.68
3. Prohibition of all forms of slavery and human trafficking
83. The Government is taking action to prevent, suppress and punish trafficking in
persons. The crime of trafficking in persons is penalized under article 172 of the Criminal
Code, and in August 2019 amendments were made to the Act on the Fight against Trafficking
in Persons and the Creation of the National Coalition against the Smuggling of Migrants and
Trafficking in Persons69 that expanded the notion of exploitation by illicit organ harvesting
beyond the definition contained in the Palermo Protocol in order to provide a more effective
tool for combating this crime.70
GE.24-15566 9

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
84. The National Policy against Trafficking in Persons is in place for the period 2020–
2030.71 The National Strategic Plan of the National Coalition against the Smuggling of
Migrants and Trafficking in Persons 2022–2027 was adopted more recently, and the
Guidelines for Inter-Agency Coordination on Offences of Human Trafficking and Migrant
Smuggling were adopted in 2023.72
85. The National Coalition against the Smuggling of Migrants and Trafficking in Persons
is tasked with rescuing and protecting victims and providing the judicial authorities with the
core evidence necessary for the prosecution of the alleged culprits. The Immediate Response
Team is a specialized inter-agency body responsible for arranging primary care measures for
victims of human trafficking and their dependants. The Crime Victim Support and Protection
Office of the Public Prosecution Service also provides care for victims.
86. Steps that have been taken to ensure the care and protection of victims of human
trafficking include the development of a strategy for comprehensive care and the mobilization
of support resources for victims and survivors of trafficking in persons and their dependants,
which has proved to be an example of the inter-agency coordination of a comprehensive
approach to victims. In addition, the Costa Rican Social Insurance Fund has a protocol for
the provision of comprehensive care in the health service, and there is a comprehensive care
model for victims and survivors of trafficking in persons.
C. Economic, social and cultural rights
1. Right to work
87. Guaranteeing the right to work entails ensuring equal access for all persons, equal pay
for women and men, an equitable distribution of parental responsibilities and shared
responsibility for care.
88. Since 2022, Act No. 10159 and Act No. 10211 have enshrined the right to paternity
leave in the public and private sector.73 In 2023, records indicate that 10,983 fathers were
granted paternity leave, a number equivalent to 0.8 paternity leaves for each maternity leave.
89. Improvements to working conditions and access to work have been promoted through
the following legal amendments:
• Amendment to the Act on the Promotion of Social Equality of Women (No. 7142),
providing for equal pay for women and men in the public and private sectors for work
of equal value74
• Dual Education and Vocational Training Act75
• Labour Code amendments to regulate night work for women workers76
• Labour Code amendments concerning freedom of choice of employment for women,
which outlawed discrimination against women of working age in certain sectors of
the economy77
• Act promoting a culture of reporting sexual harassment, which extended the period
for filing a complaint from two to eight years78
90. The Central Bank has created a satellite account to measure the economic value of the
unpaid domestic work that household members do for their own subsistence. The account
serves as a valuable tool for identifying inequalities in this area and designing strategies to
promote labour market access for women.79
91. The Government has initiated action to eliminate the discriminatory wage gap in
domestic service by means of differentiated annual salary adjustments. The process will
conclude in 2034, when the pay of domestic workers will be equal to that of other unskilled
workers.80
2. Right to receive care
92. The country is working towards a reorganization of care benefits and support services
that, adopting a perspective of shared social responsibilities, gender equality, human rights
10 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
and citizen participation, will change how responsibilities are shared by the State, the market,
households and communities.81
93. The National Care and Support System for Older Persons in Situations of
Dependency,82 which was created in 2022, will coordinate the general and specialized care
services provided to people requiring care by public and private institutions and by other
caregivers.83
94. The National Care Policy 2021–2031, which is designed to ensure the well-being of
older adults with disabilities or chronic diseases who need support, covers the organization
and coordination of services, including telecare, home care, day-care centres and long-stay
residential homes. 84 Home-based services are prioritized with a view to avoiding
institutionalization and fostering personal autonomy.
95. The National Childcare and Child Development Network, established by law in 2021,
is the coordinator of the Child Development and Comprehensive Care Centres that provide
services for children living in poverty or situations of social or economic vulnerability who
require support in the child development process, thus allowing for the labour market
insertion and continuing education of their parents.85
3. Right to water
96. The right to water, a fundamental and inalienable human right and a common good
essential for life, is recognized in the Constitution, as amended in 2020.86 By stipulating that
water is a national asset, this constitutional recognition serves to protect and guarantee the
right of access to drinking water.87
97. In 2023, 95.6 per cent of the Costa Rican population had access to quality drinking
water, 3.2 percentage points more than in 2018.88
98. One challenge the country faces is sanitation. The Costa Rican Institute of Aqueducts
and Sewers, together with the Administrative Associations of Community Aqueducts and
Sewers and other stakeholders, is taking action to address this challenge, including by
improving drinking water services and the collection and treatment of wastewater in priority
areas, in accordance with the Institutional Strategic Plan 2022–2026.89
99. As citizen participation is an important component of this action, the National Water
Governance Mechanism90 was established to provide a permanent platform for dialogue and
interaction with civil society, sector organizations, academia and the general public and thus
to help ensure the protection and sustainability of the water supply.91
100. The National Water Policy and the National Integrated Water Resource Management
Plan are currently being updated, drawing on data from the national information system for
the integrated management of water resources, which was created in 2019 with a mandate to
serve as a universally accessible repository for all information on surface and groundwater
resources.92
4. Right to health
101. The right to health has been enshrined in constitutional jurisprudence as a fundamental
right and a common good of public interest protected by the State. Health-related data for
Costa Rica show that life expectancy at birth in 2023 was 80.3 years, that 95 per cent of the
population is covered by social insurance and that, in 2020, public spending on health
accounted for 5.6 per cent of gross domestic product and 25.17 per cent of total public
spending.93
102. The National Health Policy 2023–2033 and the related action plan are guided by the
principles of universal access and intersectionality and life course and social participation
perspectives.94
103. The rate of teenage pregnancy, which is a product of improper relationships, abuse or
a lack of information, fell from 19 per cent in 2012 to 9 per cent in 2021. The Government
remains focused on this issue, and one of the goals of the National Strategic Plan for
Adolescent Health 2021–2030 is to bring the proportion of births to teenage mothers down
to 2 per cent by 2030.95
GE.24-15566 11

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
104. In Costa Rica, the only justification for terminating a pregnancy is a risk to the life or
health of the mother. A technical standard for the medical procedure referred to in article 121
of the Criminal Code, on permissible abortion,96 was promulgated in December 2019, and
there is also a clinical protocol for the comprehensive care of women affected by early
gestational loss. Both instruments are of mandatory application and have been circulated to
health personnel nationwide.97
105. The 72-hour protocol is applied in cases of sexual violence, and the Costa Rican Social
Insurance Fund has issued guidelines for emergency contraception.98
106. The Mental Health Act of 202399 will help strengthen the national mental health
system, setting out a care model built on awareness-raising, prevention, rehabilitation and
reintegration and a community-based approach.100
107. In November 2023, the Menstruation and Justice Act was passed.101 The Act
recognizes the rights of all menstruating women and provides for measures to ensure access
to hygiene products and the publication of statistics on menstrual hygiene and poverty.102
5. Right to education
108. Despite significant progress in formal education attendance, with 95.1 per cent of
children and teenagers aged from 5 to 18 years old in school in 2023, average years of
schooling reaching 8.8 years by 2020 and a literacy rate of 99.5 per cent in 2021, and towards
universal primary education and compulsory secondary education (known as diversified
education in Costa Rica), ensuring student retention and reintegration is a challenge for the
country.103
109. The schools with the highest rates of educational exclusion are being prioritized in the
application of the protocols for action formulated by the Unit for Educational Retention,
Reintegration and Success, which is working in coordination with regional educational
retention teams.104
110. Using the basic education and educational resources management system known as
the SABER Platform, from which accurate year-on-year data on exclusion can be obtained
based on the register of nominal enrolment, the authorities are able to generate data on
students’ circumstances and monitor their educational progress.105
111. The programme Big-Opportunity Schools was created with a view to strengthening
the capacities of educational institutions and implementing actions and strategies conducive
to student retention in selected State secondary schools located in areas with high levels of
social vulnerability.106
112. A key advance has been the incorporation of integrated relationships and sexuality
study programmes in the third cycle and diversified education curricula. Technical guidelines
for developing the integrated relationships and sexuality study programme from an
intercultural perspective are currently being drafted.107
D. Groups requiring special protection
1. Women and girls
113. Costa Rica has two policies – the National Policy for Effective Equality between
Women and Men 2018–2030 and the National Policy for Addressing and Preventing
Violence against Women of All Ages 2017–2032108 – intended to achieve gender equality
and eradicate all forms of violence against women. Both policies were established by law,
have remained in place despite changes of government and provide for comprehensive,
coordinated State intervention over the long term. The authorities are currently implementing
the second action plan giving effect to the National Policy for Effective Equality, covering
the years to 2026,109 and a five-year plan giving effect to the National Policy for Addressing
and Preventing Violence against Women to 2027.110
114. There have been significant legislative developments, including:
• The Act against sexual harassment in the street111
12 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
• Amendments to the Violence against Women Act and the Criminal Code112
• The Act establishing the notion of extended femicide113
• The Act on women’s rights to qualified, dignified and respectful care during
pregnancy, childbirth and the postpartum period and on newborn care114
• Consolidation of the Operational Support Centre for Victims of Domestic Violence
and Violence against Women and recognition of support services for victims of
violence against women as an essential service115
• The Act on comprehensive reparation for survivors of femicide116
115. A gender road map to facilitate access to justice for women under the new regionalized
model has been adopted. Thirty-two “purple points” have been opened, covering all parts of
the country, to provide psychological first aid to women experiencing violence, with a special
emphasis on workplace, sexual and street harassment.117
116. Under an initiative intended to involve society as a whole in the search for solutions,
safe spaces have been established, in partnership with previously identified businesses, to
serve as shelters for women facing violence or other dangerous situations.118
117. Twenty-three local immediate support and follow-up committees have been
established throughout the country, providing immediate protection and support for women
at risk of femicide. The committees are composed mainly of representatives of the judiciary,
the Ministry of Public Security and National Institute for Women.119
118. Another relevant action was the adoption of a mobile care strategy designed to
facilitate access to comprehensive care services for women affected by violence who live in
areas far from urban centres and in Indigenous communities.120
119. The analytical method known as social autopsy has been applied in 28 local femicide
analysis reports. These reports include background information on the women victims, the
situation in their communities and the institutional responses, creating an important
benchmark against which to adjust the focus of institutional action and the response to
situations where there is a risk of femicide.121
120. Work to promote new perceptions of masculinity has been stepped up; legal advice
on responsible fatherhood has been made available; training and awareness-raising
campaigns have been devised;122 initiatives such as the “We Are Family” strategy have been
rolled out; and a parental training model based on play and empathetic dialogue has been
implemented in conjunction with the private sector and civil society to promote affectionate
and secure family bonds.123
121. With a view to assisting rural women, the National Institute for Women began a
process of policy regionalization that has encompassed:
• Formulating the Gender Equality Policy for Inclusive Rural Development in the Costa
Rican Agriculture, Livestock and Fisheries Sector 2020–2030 and the related action
plan,124 and monitoring their implementation
• Amendments to the regulations implementing Act No. 9036 on the Rural
Development Institute to eliminate the excessive formalities and requirements that
rural women must fulfil when presenting productive projects125
• Amendments to the Rural Development Institute’s rural credit regulations to increase
loan values, reduce interest rates and introduce a gender perspective
• Consolidation of the National Rural Women’s Forum as a forum for the exchange of
information and analysis of decision-making gaps126
2. Children, adolescents and other young persons
122. The following key legislation has been adopted to protect children’s rights and
safeguard their best interests:
GE.24-15566 13

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
• The Act on the Right to Time, which extended the statute of limitations for criminal
offences to 25 years in the case of sexual crimes against minors or persons without
volitional or cognitive capacity127
• The Act on the Prevention of Harassment of Minors by Electronic or Virtual Means
(Grooming), establishing a regulatory framework to protect children and adolescents
from cyberbullying128
• The Act to Prevent Revictimization and Safeguard the Rights of Minors in the Costa
Rican Educational System,129 intended to prevent practices that revictimize minors
and ensure their recognition as subjects of rights within the educational system’s
disciplinary regime.130
• The Act on Prevention, Early Detection and the Response to Violence against
Children and Adolescents, providing for action to prevent and ensure the early
detection of all forms of violence against children and adolescents131
123. The National Policy for Children and Adolescents 2024–2036,132 which provides the
guiding framework for State action to promote, defend and guarantee the rights of children
and adolescents, is the product of inclusive consultations involving children and adolescents
and other relevant stakeholders.133
124. The National Child Welfare Agency, the body responsible under the Constitution for
ensuring special protection for the rights of minors, is the lead agency for the aforementioned
Policy, while the National Council on Children and Adolescents is responsible for ensuring
that it is fully implemented.134
125. Costa Rica has been at the forefront of the fight against corporal punishment, and
since 2008 it has had a law prohibiting this practice that violates children’s rights.135 The
Parenting Academy Programme is a valuable means of building new parenting models in
families and promoting the dignified treatment of the child. The programme, which is
implemented throughout the country, had 6,216 participating families in 2023.136
126. Child marriage has been prohibited in Costa Rica since 2017, and forced marriage is
considered one of the reasons for which people are trafficked.137 In 2023, the National Child
Welfare Agency created an institutional protocol, with a focus on prevention, for dealing
with improper relationships and thus to ensure enforcement of the law.138
127. Costa Rica has achieved one of the lowest rates of child labour in the region as a result
of the legal prohibition of work by persons under 15 years of age and the formulation of
intersectoral policies and mechanisms. The country is committed to and has a road map for
eradicating child labour, including its worst forms, by 2025 and thus achieving target 8.7 of
the Sustainable Development Goals. The actions provided for in the road map include the
award of subsidies to help ensure that minors continue studying.139
128. The net participation rate of the population between 12 and 17 years of age was
1.3 per cent in 2023, 0.5 per cent less than in 2020. The percentage of poor households that
included poor children or adolescents who worked fell from 41.8 per cent in 2020 to
19.1 per cent in 2023.140
129. The National Commission to Combat the Commercial Sexual Exploitation of
Children and Adolescents and its subcommissions were reactivated in 2024; the first step was
the preparation of a 2024 workplan, which drew on the National Plan to Combat the
Commercial Sexual Exploitation of Children and Adolescents 2022–2025.141
130. Incentives are offered to companies in the tourism industry to adopt a code of conduct
to discourage the sexual exploitation of children and adolescents associated with travel and
tourism.142
131. Since 2020, the country has had a public policy for young persons that covers the
period to 2024;143 the policy, for which the lead agency is the Ministry of Culture and Youth,
was adopted by the National Assembly of the National Young Persons’ Advisory Network.144
14 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
3. LGTBIQ+ persons
132. The impact of the ruling of the Constitutional Chamber handed down after the
advisory opinion issued by the Inter-American Court of Human Rights is transversal to the
entire institutional framework, and although the matter is still new to the country, it has led
to positive changes to institutional efforts to safeguard the principle of non-discrimination
for all.145
133. As of May 2024, the Supreme Electoral Tribunal had registered 3,056 same-sex
marriages. In all, 1,374 people who identify as having a gender identity different from their
registered gender identity have had their names changed.146
134. There is a protocol for the response to the bullying of lesbian, gay, bisexual and
transgender students in school,147 a protocol for action in the event of discrimination against
the LGBTIQ+ population for police forces, and in the National Prison System, persons
deprived of their freedom are, as a rule, given the freedom to decide for themselves where
they will serve their sentences.148
135. The country has a protocol on comprehensive care of transgender persons and
hormone therapy in the health services network; and the variable sexual orientation and
gender identity has been included in its statistical records.149
136. The executive branch of government has a policy on eliminating discrimination
against lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender and intersex persons150 from its institutions; the
policy requires the creation of conditions conducive to the inclusion of such persons and of
institutional commissions and policies for equality that promote awareness among public
servants, encourage the development of guidelines and protocols for positive action and
contribute to a culture of tolerance and respect for human diversity. A majority of these
institutions report that they have created such commissions and taken specific steps to favour
this population group.
4. Persons with disabilities
137. Costa Rica is committed to the full inclusion of persons with disabilities, who,
according to the second national survey on disability, conducted in 2024, account for
17.2 per cent of the total population.
138. The adoption of the following reforms should be noted:
• An amendment to article 51 of the Constitution to ensure that State affords special
protection to persons with disabilities151
• The Act authorizing the award of a second housing voucher for persons with
disabilities152
• An amendment to the Act on Equal Opportunities for Persons with Disabilities under
which guarantees relating to transportation and parking for such persons are
introduced153
• The Act providing for the establishment of an office for older persons and persons
with disabilities in the municipalities to allocate resources for the action at the local
government level154
• The amendments to the implementing regulations of the Act on Equal Opportunities
for Persons with Disabilities, strengthening the provisions on the right to work and
employment155
139. The country’s National Disability Policy Action Plan 2024–2030156 informs the
pursuit of Policy objectives in all public institutions.157
140. In accordance with legislation and policy, each institution has an accessibility and
disability commission whose role is to advise the authorities and take action, based on a
human rights approach, in favour of persons with disabilities. The commissions are part of a
network, reactivated in 2022, that is coordinated and led by the National Council on Persons
with Disabilities.
GE.24-15566 15

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
141. The Inter-Agency Institute for Social Assistance offers subsidies and cash transfers to
persons with disabilities to help them meet their basic food, health, educational and other
needs, thereby enabling improvements to their quality of life.158 The Council has selective
programmes for people in situations of poverty and neglect.159
5. Older persons
142. Costa Rica is undergoing an advanced demographic transition characterized by higher
life expectancy and falling birth rates. Between 2020 and 2030, for example, the population
aged 65 and over will grow by nearly 5 per cent, and by 2050, 23.7 per cent of the population
will be 60 or older.
143. In this context, the National Council on Older Persons, the body leading the
development of policy on ageing and old age, directs the National Strategy for Healthy
Ageing Based on the Life Course 2022–2026,160 as well as the National Policy on Ageing
and Old Age 2023–2033.161 The protection and promotion of the rights of the older persons,
their economic security, good treatment and a life free from violence, and comprehensive
health and other forms of care are addressed in these instruments.
144. The programme of the network of progressive support for the comprehensive care of
older persons offers various alternatives for the home-based care and support of older
persons, especially those living in poverty or extreme poverty; these alternatives are
complemented by a range of long-stay homes and day centres.
145. Services such as the Gold Line, a telephone hotline and assistance service, provide
timely and free responses to help older people meet their needs. The Legal Defence for Older
Persons Project, developed in conjunction with the University of Costa Rica, provides various
forms of legal aid.
6. Indigenous Peoples
146. The State recognizes that there is a historical debt in connection with the full guarantee
of the rights of Indigenous Peoples; however, specific steps have been taken that reflect the
country’s commitment to the implementation of the International Labour Organization
Indigenous and Tribal Peoples Convention, 1989 (No. 169), and other relevant instruments.
147. The comprehensive mechanism for consultations with Indigenous Peoples, in place
since 2019, sets out the steps to be taken in the consultation of Indigenous Peoples in advance
of any project or administrative measure by which they may be affected, and it has led to the
creation of a protection framework consisting of:162
• An inter-agency technical committee on Indigenous affairs, responsible for drawing
up a road map for work by Indigenous Peoples and the State
• A technical unit for consultations with Indigenous Peoples, under the responsibility
of the Office of the Deputy Minister of Peace; its functions include the establishment
of local consultation units, which coordinate logistical arrangements and specialize in
Indigenous issues, and the training of persons who work with the units163
148. In 2016, in accordance with the Indigenous Peoples Act,164 the National Plan for the
Recovery of Indigenous Territories, which involved the creation of a national fund for the
payment of compensation for Indigenous territories,165 was set in motion. The first
expropriation of a non-Indigenous person occupying land in the Conte Burica Indigenous
territory since before the entry into force of the Act took place in December 2023, resulting
in the transfer of the property to the territory’s comprehensive Indigenous development
association.166
149. The following legislative developments are noteworthy:
• The Act to safeguard the Costa Rican nationality of cross-border Indigenous persons
and guarantee the integration of cross-border Indigenous persons, under which the
concept of cross-border Indigenous person, in addition to the amendments needed to
identify such persons and provide them with the legal status that will give them access
to their rights and guarantees, is incorporated into the legal system167
16 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
• The Act pursuant to which the commemoration of the International Day of Indigenous
Peoples on 9 August of every year is declared to be of public, academic and cultural
interest168
150. The development of a public policy for Indigenous Peoples for the period 2026–2036,
which will be the first application of the comprehensive mechanism and will address issues
such as the recognition of autonomy, Indigenous territoriality, self-government and the social
inclusion of Indigenous Peoples, is under way.169
151. The teaching of Indigenous languages in preschool education in Indigenous territories
is promoted within the framework of the Educational Policy for the Promotion of Languages.
The country is also in the process of drafting a protocol for population groups in respect of
which there are special and specific laws and conditions and a protocol for the provision of
services to Indigenous Peoples by the police.170
152. The Costa Rican Social Insurance Fund is making arrangements for the creation of an
institutional data catalogue that will shed light on health-care services as they pertain to
Indigenous Peoples; the catalogue has 71,700 registered Indigenous persons, a figure that
accounts for 92 per cent of the population that identifies with an Indigenous People and
territory.171
7. Persons of African descent
153. Costa Rica has taken decisive steps to recognize the contributions of persons of
African descent to economic and social development; it has also taken affirmative action to
enable their full inclusion in society and actively combat racism and racial and all other forms
of discrimination.
154. In 2021, Costa Rica hosted the first commemoration of the International Day for
People of African Descent; it also hosted the 2019 High-Level Meeting on Equality and
Human Rights for People of African Descent, “Accelerating Global Action for the
Recognition of People of African Descent in Latin America and the Caribbean,” the outcome
document of which served as a regional contribution to the Nairobi Summit on the occasion
of the twenty-fifth anniversary of the International Conference on Population and
Development.
155. The following regulatory developments should be noted:
• The Act pursuant to which August was declared the historical month of persons of
African descent in Costa Rica172
• The Act on affirmative action to be taken for the benefit of persons of African descent
for a 10-year period, under which public institutions are obliged to set aside at least
7 per cent of vacant posts a year for persons of African descent173
• An executive decree recognizing the Costa Rican population of African descent’s
identification as a tribal people in accordance with its own cultural identity, language,
historical tradition, culture and world view174
156. The National Health Policy 2023–2033 and the accompanying action plan include
specific measures for the benefit of the population of African descent, and a course on
cross-cultural matters is being developed for health personnel.175
157. The protocol for action in situations of racial discrimination and xenophobia,
developed for teaching and administrative personnel, is followed to combat racism and
discrimination in the education sector, and the variable “racial stereotypes” has been included
in in-service training plans.176
8. Migrants and refugees
158. Costa Rica, which is considered a country of transit and destination for migrants, has
historically been a host country for economic and political refugees. For Costa Rica,
integrating migrants and refugees is key to promoting social cohesion and sustainable
development.
GE.24-15566 17

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
159. Foreign nationals account for about 10 per cent of the population, one of the highest
such percentages in the region. However, the number of people transiting through the country
has increased considerably in recent years.
160. In 2021 and 2022, Costa Rica was one of the countries worldwide in which the most
applications for asylum were submitted; in 2023, it received 15,611 refugees, 195,463
asylum-seekers and 18,900 other persons in need of international protection, 133 per cent
more than in 2022, and a record 529,348 people transited through the country.
161. This situation, which has stretched the capacity of the State, led to the declaration of
a state of emergency in 2023 in a bid to meet the needs of people who were part of mixed
migration movements, build institutional response capacity and the impact of the situation
on host communities.
162. Costa Rica, which takes a human rights-based approach to the management of human
mobility, has developed innovative measures that, now recognized as best practices, are taken
within the framework of the Comprehensive Migration Policy 2024–2034177 and the National
Integration Plan 2023–2027:178
• The Labour Migration Information System, a labour registration system for migrants
that works on the basis of the availability of labour in relation to domestic demand179
• The Labour Migration Traceability System is a means of monitoring the entry, stay
and exit of migrant labour for the sectors where the demand for labour is greatest180
• The project Health Coverage and Access to Employment for Migrants, the objective
of which is to help vulnerable economic migrants and refugees with the costs of
migration documents181
• The engagement of the Special Migratory Situations Team for the identification of
and approach to persons in special migratory situations, such as minors in transit182
163. The Migrant Employability Strategy is a framework for action to promote the insertion
of migrants into the labour market, protect their labour rights and promote equal opportunities
in the labour market. The Inter-Agency Institute for Social Assistance provides services to
the refugee population; in 2023, it provided assistance amounting to a total of
1,750,939,020 colones to 4,208 refugees and asylum-seekers.
164. The development, led by the Ministry of Education, of an intercultural education
policy for the next 10 years began in 2024. An intra-agency work team for the support of
foreign students at risk of statelessness and deportees was also established.183
165. Costa Rica appreciates its joint work with cooperating organizations, civil society and
other strategic allies in addressing human mobility and reaffirms its commitment to achieving
safe, orderly and regular migration, in accordance with fundamental rights and freedoms and
under the principle of shared responsibility.
9. Persons living with HIV
166. Costa Rica has an ongoing commitment, reflected in steps such as the comprehensive
reform of the General Act on HIV/AIDS that was completed to incorporate improved human
rights standards and take account of scientific progress, to the comprehensive care of people
living with HIV.184
167. The National Council for Comprehensive HIV/AIDS Care follows up on the National
HIV Policy and Strategic Plan 2021–2026,185 emphasizing the elimination of stigma and
discrimination against people with HIV in the workplace.186
168. The steps that have been taken include the Combination HIV Prevention Strategy for
men who have sex with men and transgender women, in addition to the organization of
educational and health promotion activities.
169. The consolidation of the combination HIV prevention model in 2023, which was led
by civil society organizations, including women living with HIV, and has been assigned a
budget by the health system, is particularly noteworthy.187
18 GE.24-15566

A/HRC/WG.6/47/CRI/1
170. The country also has regulations and guidelines that provide a solid framework for
HIV care, including access to services free from discrimination, treatment and clinical
follow-up and dignified and respectful treatment of users; preventive measures are also taken.
IV. National priorities and follow-up
171. This report is a synthesis of the State’s good practices and its efforts to guarantee
human rights. It is also an exercise in frank and transparent reflection on the country’s
situation that makes it possible to identify significant challenges.
172. Persistent poverty, inequality and exclusion, coupled with the growing impact of
climate change on people’s well-being, interfere with the full enjoyment of human rights and
are pending challenges for the country.
173. The country is considering and seeking to address the significant crisis of violence it
is experiencing, including the upsurge in gender-based violence against women and girls,
notwithstanding the reduction of the fiscal space caused by the need to stabilize the public
finances.
174. This situation makes it even more urgent to strengthen prevention in social
programmes, a necessary measure in terms of human rights and the efficient use of public
resources.
175. The virtues of the country’s strong social programmes for people in vulnerable
situations have been recognized. The need for more efficient intervention, however, including
by ensuring that programmes have shared goals and targets, has also become apparent.
176. It is necessary to move towards an integrated national system that aligns available
information with public policies and budget planning and involves long-term monitoring of
risks to ensure public intervention is effective.
177. Costa Rica is committed to formulating a plan for action in follow-up to the fourth
cycle of the universal periodic review with the involvement of civil society; the results of the
consultation conducted for this report have been organized to that end.
178. Other commitments made by the country, including in the framework of the
seventy-fifth anniversary of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, will be presented in
due course.
179. With a view to the future, Costa Rica reaffirms its commitment to human rights for
all people and to working hand in hand with all social actors, including international actors,
to address its priorities and challenges.
